meta.title: Second-Hand Play
meta.description: Second hand low, cover an honor with an honor

blocks.content:
## When Declarer Leads Toward Dummy

In third-hand play, your partner led and you responded. In **second-hand play**, declarer (or dummy) leads, and you're the next to play — before seeing what the other defender or declarer will do.

The general rule:

> **Second hand low.**

When declarer leads toward dummy (or dummy leads toward declarer), play low smoothly. Don't commit your high cards early.

## Why Play Low?

Playing low forces declarer to make a decision before seeing your partner's card. If you rush to play high, you might be doing declarer's work for them.

### Example: Second Hand Low Wins

```
         ♠ K J 3 (dummy)
   ♠ A 7 4 (you)          ♠ T 8 5 (partner)
         ♠ Q 9 6 2 (declarer)
```

Declarer leads the ♠2 toward dummy. If you play the **♠A**, dummy plays low, and declarer later finesses through your partner to win both the ♠K and ♠J — three spade tricks.

But if you play **low** (♠4), declarer must guess. If declarer plays the ♠J, partner's hand doesn't matter — the Jack wins. But declarer gets only 2 tricks. If declarer plays the ♠K, your Ace captures it later — again, only 2 tricks for declarer instead of 3.

By playing low, you force declarer into a guess and save your Ace to capture an honor.

### Another Example

```
         ♦ Q 5 3 (dummy)
   ♦ K 8 4 (you)          ♦ J 9 6 (partner)
         ♦ A T 7 2 (declarer)
```

Declarer leads the ♦2 toward dummy's ♦Q. If you play the **♦K**, declarer's Ace wins later and the Queen is free — three tricks for declarer.

If you play **low**, declarer plays the ♦Q, which wins. But now declarer leads back toward the Ace — and your ♦K captures a small card on the next round. Declarer gets only 2 tricks instead of 3.

## The Exception: Cover an Honor with an Honor

When declarer leads **an honor** (J, Q, K) and you have a higher honor, the rule changes:

> **Cover an honor with an honor.**

The purpose: by playing your honor on declarer's honor, you force declarer to use **two** high cards to win **one** trick. This promotes your partner's intermediate cards.

### Example: Covering Promotes Partner's Cards

```
         ♠ A T 5 (dummy)
   ♠ Q 7 3 (you)          ♠ 9 8 6 4 (partner)
         ♠ K J 2 (declarer)
```

Declarer leads the ♠J from hand. Should you cover with the Queen?

**Yes!** Play the ♠Q. Dummy wins the ♠A, but now declarer's remaining ♠K faces your absent Queen and partner's ♠9864. The 9 and 8 are now promoted after the A, K, Q, and J are all gone.

If you don't cover, declarer plays low from dummy, the Jack wins, and then the ♠K and ♠A take the next two tricks — three tricks for declarer.

### Another Example

```
         ♣ A 8 5 3 (dummy)
   ♣ K 7 4 (you)          ♣ J 9 2 (partner)
         ♣ Q T 6 (declarer)
```

Declarer leads the ♣Q. Cover with the **♣K**. Dummy's Ace wins, but now partner's ♣J9 are promoted. Without covering, declarer plays the Queen, you play low, dummy plays low, Queen wins. Then declarer leads the Ten toward dummy's Ace — three tricks.

## When NOT to Cover

Covering isn't always right. The key question is: **can covering promote anything for your side?**

### Don't Cover When Nothing Gets Promoted

```
         ♣ A K Q 3 (dummy)
   ♣ J 7 4 (you)          ♣ 9 8 2 (partner)
         ♣ T 6 5 (declarer)
```

Declarer leads the ♣T from hand. Should you cover with the Jack? **No!** Dummy has AKQ — even if you cover, declarer has four tricks anyway. Your cover gains nothing because there's nothing to promote for your side.

### Don't Cover the First of Touching Honors

If dummy has ♣QJT and leads the Queen, don't cover. Wait for the **last** of the touching honors.

```
         ♣ Q J T 3 (dummy)
   ♣ K 7 4 (you)          ♣ 9 8 6 (partner)
         ♣ A 5 2 (declarer)
```

Dummy leads the ♣Q. You can see ♣QJT — three touching honors. **Don't cover the Queen.** If you do, declarer plays the Ace, and the JT are both winners — three tricks.

Instead, play low on the Queen. Play low on the Jack. Cover the **Ten** (the last honor in sequence). Now declarer's Ace wins, but partner's ♣9 is promoted. Declarer gets only 3 tricks instead of 4.

The rule: **cover the last of the touching honors.**

## Summary

| Situation | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| Declarer leads a small card toward dummy | Play LOW |
| Declarer leads an honor you can cover | COVER (if it promotes partner's cards) |
| Declarer leads an honor but covering promotes nothing | Play LOW |
| Dummy leads the first of touching honors (QJT, JT9) | Wait — cover the LAST one |

problems.question: Declarer leads a low spade toward dummy's ♠KJ5. You hold ♠A83 as second hand. What do you play?
options: A) ♠A (grab your trick)
B) ♠8 (second hand low)
C) ♠3 (lowest card)
D) It depends on what's in dummy
problems.explanation: Second hand low! Play the ♠3 smoothly. Let declarer guess whether to play the King or Jack from dummy. Your Ace will capture whichever honor declarer plays later.

problems.question: Declarer leads the ♥J from hand. You hold ♥Q84 as second hand. Dummy has ♥A73. Should you cover?
options: A) Yes — cover an honor with an honor (play the Queen)
B) No — second hand low always
C) Only if partner has signaled
D) Play the ♥8 to signal
problems.explanation: Cover the Jack with the Queen! Dummy's Ace wins, but now partner's intermediate hearts (like the T or 9) may be promoted. Covering forces two honors for one trick.

problems.question: Dummy leads the ♣Q from ♣QJT5. You hold ♣K72. Should you cover the Queen?
options: A) Yes — always cover an honor
B) No — wait and cover the Ten (the last of the touching honors)
C) No — never cover
D) Cover only if you can see partner has something to promote
problems.explanation: Don't cover the first of touching honors. Dummy has QJT — let the Q and J go by, then cover the T (the last honor in sequence). This maximizes the chance of promoting something for your side.

problems.question: Declarer leads a low diamond toward dummy's ♦Q53. You hold ♦K84. What do you play?
options: A) ♦K (grab the trick before the Queen wins)
B) ♦4 (second hand low)
C) ♦8 (middle card)
D) ♦K only if declarer is likely to play the Queen
problems.explanation: Second hand low. If you play the King, declarer plays low from dummy and wins the Queen later — you've used your King to capture air. Play low and let declarer decide. If declarer finesses the Queen, your King captures it next time. If declarer plays low from dummy, partner might win.

problems.question: Declarer leads the ♠T from hand. Dummy has ♠AKQ3. You hold ♠J74. Should you cover?
options: A) Yes — cover an honor with an honor
B) No — dummy's AKQ means covering promotes nothing
C) Yes — always cover the Ten
D) It depends on how many spades partner has
problems.explanation: Dummy has AKQ — covering with your Jack can't promote anything. Declarer will take four tricks regardless. Save your Jack; don't waste it when covering gains nothing.

blocks.content: Second hand low is the default — don't commit your high cards before you need to. The exception: cover an honor with an honor when it will promote partner's intermediate cards. But don't cover when nothing can be promoted, and don't cover the first of touching honors — wait for the last.

reviewQuestions.question: Why do you 'cover an honor with an honor'?
options: To win the trick immediately
To force declarer to use two high cards for one trick, promoting partner's intermediates
Because you should always play your highest card
reviewQuestions.explanation: Covering forces declarer to spend two honors to win one trick, which promotes your partner's intermediate cards (like 9s and 10s) into winners.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Perfect! The goal is promotion — making partner's smaller cards good. That's the whole point of covering.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Covering is about promotion: by putting your honor on declarer's honor, you force two high cards to die on one trick, making partner's smaller cards into winners.

reviewQuestions.question: Dummy leads the ♣Q from QJT8. You hold ♣K53. Should you cover the Queen?
options: Yes — always cover an honor
No — wait and cover the last touching honor (the 10)
No — never cover dummy's leads
reviewQuestions.explanation: When dummy has touching honors (QJT), don't cover the first one. Wait and cover the last — the Ten. This maximizes your defensive potential.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Exactly! Cover the LAST of the touching honors. Covering too early wastes your King.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: When dummy has QJT, don't cover the Q. Let Q and J go by, then cover the T. Covering the last touching honor gives your side the best chance.
